summary: This Rockville first-time Democratic primary voter is now in narrow-the-ballot mode for June 23, 2026. The asks have shifted from 'tell me what's on my ballot' to 'tell me which one of these named people actually votes the way I claim I want to.' Property-tax skepticism and a desire to cut non-essential spending are the strongest priors; sanctuary-policy skepticism is a recurring secondary prior; school spending is the connective tissue between the two because it lands on property taxes. The listener wants Andrew Friedson tested specifically against the fiscal-discipline label and Greg Kramer tested specifically on whether 'votes yes on every education increase' actually holds up bill-by-bill. The new ask of the week is the Montgomery County school rating system — they want a deep dive on it, not a passing mention. Repetition complaints continue to be the #1 quality issue.
themes: Candidate-specific voting records over generalities — listener wants Friedson, Kramer, Glass, Jawando, Stewart, Crutchfield, Tichy named with bill numbers and roll-call counts, not described in general terms

Property tax as the master variable — listener treats every state and county education vote as a downstream property-tax question and expects the show to trace the chain from state floor to local Maintenance-of-Effort to their tax bill
Sanctuary-policy stance, candidate by candidate — listener wants a clean answer for each Democrat on the ballot, not category-level claims about the county
Wootton High School relocation — listener has asked three weeks running why MCPS wants to move it; this is now the local-school story they are most invested in
The MCPS new rating system — fresh ask from 2026-04-30 daily note; listener wants a structured deep dive, not a paragraph
open_questions: I need a deep dive of the new rating system of montgomery county, public school system
Is Andrew Friedson the man who does fiscal restriction?
Why do they want to close and move Wootton High School?
What is Kramer's full historical voting record, including old news and public records beyond recent sessions?
Who among the Democratic primary candidates actually opposes sanctuary policy on illegal immigration?
underserved_topics: MCPS rating / accountability system — 2026-04-30 ask has not been addressed at all yet
Crutchfield, Stewart, Tichy — the District 19 House non-incumbent filers; show has promised dossiers and not delivered
Glass and Jawando full fiscal records on par with the Friedson dossier — currently the listener has 'three Democrats agree on X' framing more than candidate-by-candidate splits
Public Election Fund participation by candidate — touched on in Friedson dossier (he opted out) but not extended to a side-by-side
Side-by-side policy comparison table for Governor, County Executive, House of Delegates D19
avoid_list: The framing "a listener asked us last week/earlier this week which Democratic primary candidates actually oppose sanctuary policy on illegal immigration" used as a setup line — appears verbatim or near-verbatim in both 2026-04-28 ep1 and 2026-04-29 ep3

The Evan Glass bill requiring background screening for DHS agents joining Montgomery County police — introduced and framed as the concrete sanctuary-policy example in 2026-04-28 ep1 and again in 2026-04-29 ep3
The ICE agent mask-ban ordinance passed by the Montgomery County Council — covered as the sanctuary-policy benchmark in 2026-04-29 ep2
"Two stories, properly worked through" / "two stories, not spreading thin across six" as an episode-framing device — used in 2026-04-29 ep1
Governor Moore's "Leave No One Behind" slogan contrasted with disability services cuts — used in 2026-04-27 ep1 and ep1.rewrite
The ACA marketplace quiet-downgrade story ("thousands of Marylanders have been quietly downgrading their health insurance plans") — introduced in 2026-04-28 ep1 and revisited in 2026-04-29 ep3

Maryland's 90-day part-time legislature explanation ("they meet in Annapolis for ninety days, the rest of the time they're lawyers, teachers, small business owners") — used in 2026-04-26 ep3 and ep3.rewrite
The wave of 25 Maryland lawmakers retiring or running for other offices framing — used in 2026-04-26 ep1, ep1.rewrite, and ep4
The Beltsville Agricultural Research Center closure story as a District 19 candidate-record hook — used in 2026-04-27 ep4 and ep4.rewrite

Listener's own frustration about promised candidate deep-dives not delivering — the show has now narrated this complaint back to the listener across at least five episodes; do not repeat it as a framing device again

Kramer 8-vote walkthrough across HB1273, HB642, HB933, HB211, HB1485 (Pension Cost Shift no), HB304, HB712, HB1102 — already laid out in full in 2026-05-02 ep1; reference summary only, do not re-read every bill
Friedson 4-no-votes on Elrich property-tax increases (2020, 2023, 2025, 2026) as the lead Friedson hook — used in 2026-05-02 deepdive_friedson; can cite once but do not lead an episode with it
Comptroller Lierman direct-care vacancy 22% vs 16% Montgomery County / state — covered in 2026-05-02 ep1; if revisited, advance the story instead of repeating the headline number
Baltimore Inner Harbor ColiMinder real-time water testing as a 'this lands on the County Executive's desk' hook — used in 2026-05-02 ep1
Luedtke endorsement of Friedson framed as 'your councilmember has endorsed' — used in 2026-05-02 deepdive_friedson
audit_notes: DB upsert into weekly_themes table not performed in this run — bash/sqlite access was unavailable. Windows-side processor should mirror this JSON into the weekly_themes row when it picks up the .done.json marker.

Window window_start 2026-04-26 reflects the available daily_notes context shipped in the briefs. If the live politics.db has additional notes dated 2026-05-01 / 2026-05-02 / 2026-05-03 that were not included in the brief, the Windows-side processor should re-run themes after merging.
